en.extract:
Henslow's sparrow (Centronyx henslowii) is a North American passerine bird in the family Passerellidae. It was named by John James Audubon in honor of John Stevens Henslow. It was originally classified in the genus Emberiza and called Henslow's bunting.


Description
Adults have streaked brown upperparts with a light brown breast with streaks, a white belly and a white throat. They have a pale stripe on the crown with a dark stripe on each side, an olive face and neck, rust-coloured wings and a short dark forked tail.
Measurements:

Length: 4.3–5.1 in (11–13 cm)
Weight: 0.4–0.5 oz (11–14 g)
Wingspan: 6.3–7.9 in (16–20 cm)


Distribution and habitat
The range and numbers of this bird are decreasing, probably due to habitat loss of the grasslands that it depends on. However, it has heavily benefited from the Conservation Reserve Program formed by the United States Department of Agriculture, which has helped to stabilize its population. Following this, it was downlisted to Least Concern from Near Threatened in 2018.
The Texas population was solely known from a 105-acre (0.42 km2) brushfield near Houston and disappeared after devegetation due to industrial development in the 1980s. It was considered a distinct subspecies (P. h. houstonensis: Arnold, 1983) but is today considered to fall into the range of variation of the nominate subspecies (Browning, 1990). Likewise, the South Dakotan population formerly known as P. h. occidentalis has been synonymized with the nominate. The only remaining subspecies generally (but not universally) accepted are the eastern Henslow's sparrow and the western Henslow's sparrow, whose ranges are for the most part separated by the Appalachian Mountains.


Diet and behavior
These birds forage on the ground, mainly eating insects (including grasshoppers and beetles), berries, and seeds. Their song is a quick se-lick.


Breeding
Their breeding habitat is shrubby fields, often wet, in southern Canada, the northeastern United States, and the midwestern United States. In 2020, the Central Kentucky Audubon Society discovered that a population of Henslow's sparrows near a winery only needed 5 acres of grassy habitat to successfully breed (previous research had suggested at least 20 to 75 acres were necessary). The nest is a well-concealed open cup on or close to the ground in a grassy location; these birds often nest in small colonies. They migrate to marshes and open pine woods in the southeastern United States.


References

Arnold, Keith A. (April 1983). "A New Subspecies of Henslow's Sparrow (Ammodramus Henslowii)" (PDF). The Auk. 100 (2): 504–505. doi:10.1093/auk/100.2.504. JSTOR 4086551.
Browning, M. Ralph (28 June 1990). "Taxa of North American birds described from 1957–1987". Proceedings of the Biological Society of Washington. 103 (2): 432–451.


Further reading

Books

Reports

Theses

Articles


External links
Henslow's sparrow species account - Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Henslow's sparrow - Ammodramus henslowii - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter
Henslow's sparrow photo gallery VIREO

Brewster, 1918 *''Ammodramus henslowii'' | range_map = Ammodramus henslowii map.svg | range_map_caption = {{leftlegend|#FF7F2A|Breeding}}{{leftlegend|#5F8DD3|Nonbreeding}} }} {{listen|filename=Ammodramus henslowii - Henslow's Sparrow XC137737.ogg|title=Song|description= |pos=right}} '''Henslow's sparrow''' ('''''Centronyx henslowii''''') is a North American [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Passerellidae]]. It was named by [[John James Audubon]] in honor of [[John Stevens Henslow]]. It was originally classified in the genus ''[[Emberiza]]'' and called ''Henslow's bunting''.{{Cite web|url=https://www.audubon.org/birds-of-america/henslows-sparrow|title=Audubon's Birds of America|website=www.audubon.org|access-date=2019-06-03}} ==Description== Adults have streaked brown upperparts with a light brown breast with streaks, a white belly and a white throat. They have a pale stripe on the crown with a dark stripe on each side, an olive face and neck, rust-coloured wings and a short dark forked tail. '''Measurements''':{{Cite web|title=Henslow's Sparrow Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Henslows_Sparrow/id|access-date=2020-09-29|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}} * '''Length''': {{convert|4.3|-|5.1|in|cm|abbr=on}} * '''Weight''': {{convert|0.4|-|0.5|oz|g|abbr=on}} * '''Wingspan''': {{convert|6.3|-|7.9|in|cm|abbr=on}} ==Distribution and habitat== The range and numbers of this bird are decreasing, probably due to habitat loss of the grasslands that it depends on. However, it has heavily benefited from the [[Conservation Reserve Program]] formed by the [[United States Department of Agriculture]], which has helped to stabilize its population. Following this, it was downlisted to [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]] from [[Near-threatened species|Near Threatened]] in 2018.{{Cite news|url=http://www.birdlife.org/worldwide/news/red-list-northern-bald-ibis-pink-pigeon-making-comeback|title=Red List: Northern Bald Ibis, Pink Pigeon making a comeback|author=BirdLife International|work=BirdLife|access-date=2018-11-23|language=en-us}} The [[Texas]] population was solely known from a {{convert|105|acre|km2|adj=on}} brushfield near [[Houston]] and disappeared after devegetation due to industrial development in the 1980s. It was considered a distinct subspecies (''P. h. houstonensis'': Arnold, 1983) but is today considered to fall into the range of variation of the nominate subspecies (Browning, 1990). Likewise, the [[South Dakota]]n population formerly known as ''P. h. occidentalis'' has been synonymized with the nominate. The only remaining subspecies generally (but not universally) accepted are the eastern Henslow's sparrow and the western Henslow's sparrow, whose ranges are for the most part separated by the [[Appalachian Mountains]]. ==Diet and behavior== These birds forage on the ground, mainly eating [[insect]]s (including grasshoppers and beetles), [[Berry|berries]], and [[seed]]s.{{Cite web |title=Henslow's Sparrow |url=https://abcbirds.org/bird/henslows-sparrow/ |access-date=2022-03-24 |website=American Bird Conservancy |language=en}} Their song is a quick ''se-lick''. ==Breeding== Their breeding habitat is shrubby fields, often wet, in southern [[Canada]], the [[northeastern United States]],{{Cite web |last=Coburn |first=Lydia |date=2022-09-14 |title=Rare Birds Sighted at Crystal Spring Farm! |url=https://www.btlt.org/rare-birds-sighted-at-crystal-spring-farm/ |access-date=2023-02-22 |website=[[Brunswick-Topsham Land Trust]] |language=en-US}} and the [[midwestern United States]].{{cite web |url=http://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Henslows_Sparrow/id |title=Henslow's Sparrow |work=All About Birds |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=2009-07-25}} In 2020, the Central Kentucky [[Audubon|Audubon Society]] discovered that a population of Henslow's sparrows near a winery only needed 5 acres of grassy habitat to successfully breed (previous research had suggested at least 20 to 75 acres were necessary).{{Cite web |last=Saleh |first=Gabrielle |date=2022-03-21 |title=How Just a Few Acres of Ideal Habitat Helped Protect Henslow's Sparrows |url=https://www.audubon.org/news/how-just-few-acres-ideal-habitat-helped-protect-henslows-sparrows |access-date=2022-03-24 |website=Audubon |language=en}}{{Cite journal |last=Brusate |first=Tony |date=November 2020 |title=Protecting Established Henslow's Sparrow Breeding Colonies Through Selective Haying |url=https://www.birdky.org/pdfs/protecting_established_henslow_breeding.pdf?emci=30580163-907f-ec11-94f6-c896650d4442&emdi=7f36a09d-6c80-ec11-94f6-c896650d4442&ceid=3648621 |journal=Kentucky Warbler |volume=96}} The nest is a well-concealed [[cup nest|open cup]] on or close to the ground in a grassy location; these birds often nest in small colonies. They [[bird migration|migrate]] to [[marsh]]es and open [[pine]] woods in the [[southeastern United States]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} *{{cite journal |last=Arnold |first=Keith A. |title=A New Subspecies of Henslow's Sparrow (''Ammodramus Henslowii'') |journal=[[The Auk]] |date = April 1983|volume=100 |issue=2 |pages=504–505 |jstor=4086551|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v100n02/p0504-p0505.pdf|doi=10.1093/auk/100.2.504 }} *{{cite journal |last=Browning |first=M.
